tei_path: /content/drive/MyDrive/content_analysis_epistemology/papers/tei/raw/psychology/Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro.tei.xml

metadata.title: #Coronavirus: Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus 2 Pandemic

abstract: In this study, a social media analysis is conducted to examine the public discourse about the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) pandemic on Twitter. In particular, this study aims to examine (a) how the number of tweets varies as a function of the timeline of the pandemic and associated measures and (b) how the content of these tweets, including displayed emotions, changes. Therefore, 373,908 tweets and retweets from Belgium were collected from February 25, 2020 to the March 30. Time series analysis, network bigrams, topic models, and emotional lexica were deployed for analysis. The results showed that significant events related to the virus correlated with an immediate increase in the number of tweets addressing them. Furthermore, the Belgian Twitter discourse was characterized by positively connoted words, which also refer to European solidarity. These findings do not only stress the relevance of Twitter as a medium for public discourse during lockdowns, but also seem to indicate that the Belgian public supports policy measures that respect solidarity in Europe.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: T he recent outbreak of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) pandemic has disrupted the public life across the world in a manner unseen since the end of the World War II. The virus, first identified in Wuhan (China) in December 2019, was recognized by the World Health Organization as a pandemic in March 2020, causing a severe threat to the health of people around the world. Given the serious public health risk, it was of critical importance for policy makers to respond early to be able to slow down the community spread of the virus and as such lower the burden on the health care system. Following the examples of other countries, the Belgian government decided in several stages to take far-reaching measures to curtail public life and urge people to stay at home to further decrease the spread of the virus. In this study, a social media analysis was conducted to examine the public discourse about the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic on Twitter. In particular, this study wants to answer two research questions: (1) how does the number of tweets vary as a function of the timeline of the pandemic and associated measures and (2) how does the content of these tweets, including displayed emotions, vary over time. Gaining profound insight into how the public perceives this pandemic and how it reacts to the measures declared by the government is of utmost importance for policy makers, health workers, and stakeholders who communicate to the public during infectious disease outbreaks.

Agenda-setting theory describes the ability of media to alter the salience of issues on the public agenda. 1 According to basic agenda setting, issues that get a lot of attention in the media are considered more important by the public. 2 It has been argued that nowadays, Twitter has at least as much influence on the public agenda as newspapers. 3,4 During health crises, Twitter gives users the possibility to express opinions and share breaking news. 5 Initial studies on tweets during emerging epidemics have found that Twitter can be used to identify public concerns 6 or even trace infectious activity. 7,8 Furthermore, it can be an indicator for the public risk perception of an infection. 9 Although these initial studies report on the use of Twitter during infectious disease outbreaks such as Zika, 6,9 bird flu (H7N9), 5 and swine flu (H1N1), 7,8 examining the public discourse on Twitter during the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic is particularly relevant given that the scale of it was unseen and it had an enormous impact on the daily lives of billions of people around the world.
sections.text:
T he recent outbreak of the severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) pandemic has disrupted the public life across the world in a manner unseen since the end of the World War II. The virus, first identified in Wuhan (China) in December 2019, was recognized by the World Health Organization as a pandemic in March 2020, causing a severe threat to the health of people around the world. Given the serious public health risk, it was of critical importance for policy makers to respond early to be able to slow down the community spread of the virus and as such lower the burden on the health care system. Following the examples of other countries, the Belgian government decided in several stages to take far-reaching measures to curtail public life and urge people to stay at home to further decrease the spread of the virus. In this study, a social media analysis was conducted to examine the public discourse about the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic on Twitter. In particular, this study wants to answer two research questions: (1) how does the number of tweets vary as a function of the timeline of the pandemic and associated measures and (2) how does the content of these tweets, including displayed emotions, vary over time. Gaining profound insight into how the public perceives this pandemic and how it reacts to the measures declared by the government is of utmost importance for policy makers, health workers, and stakeholders who communicate to the public during infectious disease outbreaks.

Agenda-setting theory describes the ability of media to alter the salience of issues on the public agenda. 1 According to basic agenda setting, issues that get a lot of attention in the media are considered more important by the public. 2 It has been argued that nowadays, Twitter has at least as much influence on the public agenda as newspapers. 3,4 During health crises, Twitter gives users the possibility to express opinions and share breaking news. 5 Initial studies on tweets during emerging epidemics have found that Twitter can be used to identify public concerns 6 or even trace infectious activity. 7,8 Furthermore, it can be an indicator for the public risk perception of an infection. 9 Although these initial studies report on the use of Twitter during infectious disease outbreaks such as Zika, 6,9 bird flu (H7N9), 5 and swine flu (H1N1), 7,8 examining the public discourse on Twitter during the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic is particularly relevant given that the scale of it was unseen and it had an enormous impact on the daily lives of billions of people around the world.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: For this study, corona-related tweets were collected from February 25, 2020, to March 30. The first patient in Belgium tested positive on SARS-CoV-2 on February 4; however, press attention only started to rise after *100 Belgians were quarantined in a hotel on Tenerife, from February 25 onward. 10,11 This event marks the start of our data collection. Thus, the data set in this study provides an overview of corona-related tweets during the unfolding of the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic in Belgium.

At the start of the data collection, it appeared that the hashtags ''coronavirus,'' ''covid19,'' ''coronavid19,'' and ''corona'' were used most frequently in Belgium. Resultantly, these hashtags were used to collect the data. However, starting on March 11, ''Coronavirusoutbreak,'' ''COVID19BE,'' and ''coronabelgie'' were also regarded because Twitter displayed them as trending. Tweets were collected through the streaming Twitter API access in combination with the twitteR 12 package in R.

All tweets and retweets posted in Belgium in Dutch, French, and English languages were included in the data collection, although the streaming Twitter API only allowed us to capture the first 128 characters of a sample of actual posted tweets. In total, the scraping procedure captured 373,908 tweets and retweets of which 71,481 were original tweets. A subsample of 31,454 tweets were written in English, 15,936 were in French, and 24,091 were in Dutch.

Thus, the data set in this study provides an overview of corona-related tweets during the unfolding of the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic in Belgium.

At the start of the data collection, it appeared that the hashtags ''coronavirus,'' ''covid19,'' ''coronavid19,'' and ''corona'' were used most frequently in Belgium. Resultantly, these hashtags were used to collect the data. However, starting on March 11, ''Coronavirusoutbreak,'' ''COVID19BE,'' and ''coronabelgie'' were also regarded because Twitter displayed them as trending. All tweets and retweets posted in Belgium in Dutch, French, and English languages were included in the data collection, although the streaming Twitter API only allowed us to capture the first 128 characters of a sample of actual posted tweets. In total, the scraping procedure captured 373,908 tweets and retweets of which 71,481 were original tweets. A subsample of 31,454 tweets were written in English, 15,936 were in French, and 24,091 were in Dutch.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: Before analysis, tweets were cleaned and stemmed using the text mining 13 and tidytext 14 packages. In particular, stop words, punctuation, and other special signs such as smileys, hashtags, and slashes were removed. Porter's Snowball stemmer 15 was applied to reduce the processed words to their word stems (e.g., reducing ''washing'' to ''wash''). To answer RQ1, the number of tweets and retweets were computed on an hourly basis for the given time frame. To tackle RQ2, three analytical techniques were applied. First, network bigrams examined which pairs of words frequently appear together and how they relate to each other. Second, to analyze the emotional connotation of the words, an emotion lexicon based on mechanical turk 16 was used. Third, machine learning-based topic models were constructed to show the themes that the tweets addressed frequently. Latent Dirichlet allocation was used for that purpose as it is implemented in the topic models package 17 in R. The R-code used for the analysis is available in Supplementary Appendix.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: Figure 1 shows the number of English, Dutch, and French tweets and retweets on the given hashtags aggregated per hour as received by the streaming API. The first peak occurs on March 2. On this day, the number of infections jumped from two to eight in Belgium. 18 Afterward the number of tweets decreased to its initial level again until another spike occurred on Monday March 9 when stock markets crashed worldwide, leading to a loss of the BEL20 of about eight percent. The first death resulting from SARS-CoV-2 in Belgium was announced on the 11th of March by the Belgian Minister of Health. 19 The government announced its decision to close schools on March 12, 20 which correlated with an increase in the number of tweets. It was communicated that the schools should stay closed at least until the end of the Easter holidays on April 20. On March 14 and 15, the number of tweets dropped again. This is because the number of tweets is lower on the weekends. This trend of lower tweets on weekends continues throughout the time series. On March 17, stricter social distancing measures were introduced, 21 meaning that leaving the place of residence was restricted to essential activities such as grocery shopping, medical treatments, and exercise. Furthermore, it was generally restricted to meet in groups larger than two, except for direct relatives living in the same household. FIG. 1. Number of tweets and retweets over time.

hour when the Belgian prime minister publicly announced the implementation of that measure. The highest number of tweets could be registered on March 19, when Michel Barnier, a wellknown French politician, announced through Twitter that he had tested positive for the virus. Another rise in the number of tweets occurred 1 week later when the U.S. congress approved a 2 trillion dollar economical stimulus. 22 On March 30, a high number of tweets addressed that the Hungarian government passed an emergency bill, resulting in a variety of executive rights for the president.

It can also be stated that the number of tweets increased rapidly after the mentioned happenings took place. Most of the tweets were sent within 2 hours after the occasion. The exception was the U.S. approval of the 2 trillion dollar stimulus that became public between 5 and 6 a.m. Belgian time that was associated with a later spike of tweets between 10 and 11 a.m.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: For further semantic analysis, we removed the retweets from the data set and focused on tweets in English language. We did so because this was the most frequent language used within the captured tweets. To answer RQ2 and gain insight into the content of the tweets, a network of bigrams was computed. The network shows which terms frequently occur together. By applying that technique, we can learn how different words are combined within tweets. As shown in Figure 2, some bigrams appear to be largely unrelated to other word pairs, for example, ''toilet paper'' and ''South Korea,'' The main network of bigrams is centered around the words ''covid19'' and ''coronavirus,'' containing words such as ''fight,'' ''spread,'' ''response,'' ''test,'' and ''lockdown.'' The network indicates some positive themes within tweets. For instance, the word ''Italian'' occurs frequently together with ''support'' and ''friend.'' Also ''stay'' and ''inside'' are connected to ''safe.'' Although the word ''death'' occurs in the network, there were no agonizing words such as ''fear,'' ''disaster,'' or similar frequently used.

To gain a further understanding of word relations within the tweets, topic modeling was deployed. Its aim was to reveal several latent topics and the likelihood of words to appear within these topics. To select the appropriate number of topics, we followed the guidelines of Maier et al. 23 and not only used different metrics [24][25][26] but also 10-fold crossvalidation. The indices showed that at least six different topics should be modeled.

Figure 3 displays the word stems on its y-axis and the density of terms within that topic on its x-axis, represented by beta. It is not surprising that the terms ''coronavirus'' and ''covid19'' have a high likelihood to occur in each of the six topics. However, all of these topics stress slightly different aspects of the discourse. For instance, a tweet addressing the first topic is likely to contain the term ''crisis'' (b crisis1 = 0.0093), ''country'' (b countri1 = 0.0066), ''pandemic'' (b pan- TWITTER DISCOURSE ON SARS-COV-2 pandemic. Several aspects of containing it are discussed in the tweets such as ''the need for further testing'' in topic 6 and ''the importance of time'' in topic 3. Furthermore, topics 2 and 5 stress the need for international support for countries that have been severely hit by the pandemic, such as Italy.

Furthermore, topics 2 and 5 stress the need for international support for countries that have been severely hit by the pandemic, such as Italy.
sections.preview_head: For further semantic analysis, we removed the retweets from the data set and focused on tweets in English language. We did so because this was the most frequent language used within the captured tweets. To answer RQ2 and gain insight into the content of the tweets, a network of bigrams was computed. The network shows which terms frequently occur together.
sections.preview_tail: For instance, a tweet addressing the first topic is likely to contain the term ''crisis'' (b crisis1 = 0.0093), ''country'' (b countri1 = 0.0066), ''pandemic'' (b pan- TWITTER DISCOURSE ON SARS-COV-2 pandemic. Several aspects of containing it are discussed in the tweets such as ''the need for further testing'' in topic 6 and ''the importance of time'' in topic 3. Furthermore, topics 2 and 5 stress the need for international support for countries that have been severely hit by the pandemic, such as Italy.

sections.paper_id: Kurten and Beullens - 2021 - #Coronavirus Monitoring the Belgian Twitter Discourse on the Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome Coro
paragraphs: Finally, the emotions associated with the words within the tweets were examined. They were coded by using an emotional lexicon by Mohammad and Turney. 16 The results are shown in Figure 4. Every layer within the area plot represents the relative amount of a given emotion within original tweets on a daily basis.

Novel to this analysis is the insight which emotions were expressed the most frequently throughout the tweets. On average, the tweets contained terms associated with ''trust'' the most often ( x trust = 28.9 percent of all words, SD trust = 0.03), followed by ''anticipation'' ( x anticipation = 17.5 percent, SD anticipation = 0.02), and ''fear'' ( x fear = 17.0 percent, SD fear = 0.02). Terms that were associated with ''anger'' ( x anger = 7.8 percent, SD anger = 0.01), ''surprise'' ( x surprise = 6.4 percent, SD surprise = 0.01), and ''disgust'' ( x disgust = 4.4 percent, SD disgust = 0.01) occurred the least frequently. In addition, the analysis showed that trust had the highest volatility among the emotions. This means that when the number of tweets increased, the proportion of trustful tweets increased more strongly than tweets expressing different emotions. The proportion of trustful tweets ranged from 21.5 percent on February 29 to 37.1 percent on March 2, meaning that the highest proportion of tweets containing trust was obtained right after the first jump in infections. Trustful tweets decreased after both the stock market crash on March 9 and the decision to close schools on March 12. Nevertheless, trustful tweets then rose to their second highest value on March 16 (33.8 percent). When stricter social distancing measures were introduced on the following day, the proportion of trustful tweets decreased only slightly on March 17 (30.7 percent) and March 18 (29.5 percent). When the number of tweets peaked again on the 26th, a higher proportion of trust could be detected (32 percent), decreasing in the following days. FIG. 4. Emotions within tweets over time.
120 KURTEN AND BEULLENS Discussion

The SARS-CoV-2 pandemic was the cause for a curtailment of the public life in Belgium and many countries worldwide. Governments introduced strict social distancing measures and prohibited unnecessary displacements. In these situations, social media remains one of the few areas in which public discourse is still possible. Looking at tweets from the first month of the unfolding of the pandemic, this study wanted to (a) analyze how the number of pandemicrelated tweets changed as a function of critical incidents and (b) examine the content of these tweets in terms of its semantic and emotional connotation. This is especially relevant as it has been shown that Twitter is an important source of information in times of crises. [27][28][29] As such, this study provides a unique insight into the topics that were discussed by the public during the start of the SARS-CoV-2 outbreak, in the way the discourse was held, and how social media users responded to the measures taken by the government.

Time series analysis showed that significant events related to the virus resulted in an immediate increase in the number of tweets addressing them. Similar patterns have been observed in past research. Szomszor et al., 30 for instance, reported substantial increases in the number of tweets in response to a natural hazard, Terpstra et al. 28 made similar observations related to the swine flu pandemic in 2009. This study showed that the number of tweets peaked in the hours after the governments' declaration of the measurement taken to prevent the further spread of the SARS-CoV-2, including the closing of the schools and stricter social distancing measures. In addition, high number of tweets was, among others, associated with the worldwide crash of stock markets on March 9 and Michel Barnier's confirmed infection on March 19. These findings further stress the relevance of Twitter as a medium for public discourse during lockdowns and isolation measures, and its potential to influence the salience of the topics on the public agenda. The pace with which the discourse unfolded on Twitter after an event provided some hints for Twitter's role in the agenda-setting process. Individuals' appear to turn to Twitter to communicate important events during crises. This effect could have been reinforced by the Belgian lockdown, which made it harder for citizens to discuss relevant issues offline. This implies that examining these tweets provides important information for policy makers, as the tweets help them understand the public response to policy measures.

The content of the tweets was by definition centered on the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic, but stressed different aspects. First, the bigram network and the topic models showed that the Belgian Twitter discourse was characterized by positive connoted words referring to European solidarity. For instance, ''support'' and ''European'' were prominent terms within the topics, as well as ''friend,'' ''Italian,'' and ''support.'' These findings seem to indicate that the Belgian public supports policy measures that respect solidarity with European countries suffering from more severe consequences of the pandemic.

The conclusion that the Belgian Twitter users convey trust in their ability to overcome the pandemic is further supported by the results from the analysis of emotions within tweets. Not only was the most frequently expressed emotion ''trust,'' but also the relative proportion of trust increased, when the number of original tweets rose. Although previous research pointed out that Twitter can potentially create panic, for example, during the Ebola epidemic, 31 the results seem to indicate that the proportion of fear within tweets was rather low. These findings provide some evidence for Twitter's attribute agenda-setting capabilities. Despite rising case numbers, the discourse emphasized trustful attributes of the topics on public agenda. We conclude that positive emotions within the Belgian Twitter discourse have the potential to increase the importance of positive aspects of the pandemicrelated agenda. In addition, the trust of the Belgian public in the ability to cope with the pandemic remained largely intact.

Like all research, this study has a number of limitations. First, the streaming Twitter API only provided a sample of up to one percent of all tweets. Although it seems likely that the sample is representative, Twitter does not disclose their sampling procedure and, therefore, it remains unknown. Second, although English, French, and Belgian sample data were collected, the analysis of the tweets content only regarded tweets in English. This might limit the possibility to infer how the Belgian Twitter users felt as a whole. Third, Belgian Twitter users are not an accurate representation of the Belgian public, and the tweets must be regarded as an indicative sample. Fourth, it is not clear to which extent the sample is subject to automated posting of tweets and not activity. These issues are a common concern within the use of Twitter data. 32 Despite its limitations, this study adds to the current state-ofthe-art Twitter analysis by being the first to examine the public discourse on Twitter in the first month of the SARS-CoV-2 crises. As such, it provides important insight into how the Belgian public responded to the far-reaching measures taken by the government to curtail public life.
sections.text:
Finally, the emotions associated with the words within the tweets were examined. They were coded by using an emotional lexicon by Mohammad and Turney. 16 The results are shown in Figure 4. Every layer within the area plot represents the relative amount of a given emotion within original tweets on a daily basis.

Novel to this analysis is the insight which emotions were expressed the most frequently throughout the tweets. On average, the tweets contained terms associated with ''trust'' the most often ( x trust = 28.9 percent of all words, SD trust = 0.03), followed by ''anticipation'' ( x anticipation = 17.5 percent, SD anticipation = 0.02), and ''fear'' ( x fear = 17.0 percent, SD fear = 0.02). Terms that were associated with ''anger'' ( x anger = 7.8 percent, SD anger = 0.01), ''surprise'' ( x surprise = 6.4 percent, SD surprise = 0.01), and ''disgust'' ( x disgust = 4.4 percent, SD disgust = 0.01) occurred the least frequently. In addition, the analysis showed that trust had the highest volatility among the emotions. This means that when the number of tweets increased, the proportion of trustful tweets increased more strongly than tweets expressing different emotions. The proportion of trustful tweets ranged from 21.5 percent on February 29 to 37.1 percent on March 2, meaning that the highest proportion of tweets containing trust was obtained right after the first jump in infections. Trustful tweets decreased after both the stock market crash on March 9 and the decision to close schools on March 12. Nevertheless, trustful tweets then rose to their second highest value on March 16 (33.8 percent). When stricter social distancing measures were introduced on the following day, the proportion of trustful tweets decreased only slightly on March 17 (30.7 percent) and March 18 (29.5 percent). When the number of tweets peaked again on the 26th, a higher proportion of trust could be detected (32 percent), decreasing in the following days. FIG. 4. Emotions within tweets over time.

120 KURTEN AND BEULLENS Discussion

The SARS-CoV-2 pandemic was the cause for a curtailment of the public life in Belgium and many countries worldwide. This is especially relevant as it has been shown that Twitter is an important source of information in times of crises. [27][28][29] As such, this study provides a unique insight into the topics that were discussed by the public during the start of the SARS-CoV-2 outbreak, in the way the discourse was held, and how social media users responded to the measures taken by the government.

Similar patterns have been observed in past research. Szomszor et al., 30 for instance, reported substantial increases in the number of tweets in response to a natural hazard, Terpstra et al. 28 made similar observations related to the swine flu pandemic in 2009. This study showed that the number of tweets peaked in the hours after the governments' declaration of the measurement taken to prevent the further spread of the SARS-CoV-2, including the closing of the schools and stricter social distancing measures. In addition, high number of tweets was, among others, associated with the worldwide crash of stock markets on March 9 and Michel Barnier's confirmed infection on March 19. These findings further stress the relevance of Twitter as a medium for public discourse during lockdowns and isolation measures, and its potential to influence the salience of the topics on the public agenda. The pace with which the discourse unfolded on Twitter after an event provided some hints for Twitter's role in the agenda-setting process. Individuals' appear to turn to Twitter to communicate important events during crises. This effect could have been reinforced by the Belgian lockdown, which made it harder for citizens to discuss relevant issues offline. This implies that examining these tweets provides important information for policy makers, as the tweets help them understand the public response to policy measures.

The content of the tweets was by definition centered on the SARS-CoV-2 pandemic, but stressed different aspects. First, the bigram network and the topic models showed that the Belgian Twitter discourse was characterized by positive connoted words referring to European solidarity. For instance, ''support'' and ''European'' were prominent terms within the topics, as well as ''friend,'' ''Italian,'' and ''support.'' These findings seem to indicate that the Belgian public supports policy measures that respect solidarity with European countries suffering from more severe consequences of the pandemic.

The conclusion that the Belgian Twitter users convey trust in their ability to overcome the pandemic is further supported by the results from the analysis of emotions within tweets. Not only was the most frequently expressed emotion ''trust,'' but also the relative proportion of trust increased, when the number of original tweets rose. Although previous research pointed out that Twitter can potentially create panic, for example, during the Ebola epidemic, 31 the results seem to indicate that the proportion of fear within tweets was rather low. These findings provide some evidence for Twitter's attribute agenda-setting capabilities. Despite rising case numbers, the discourse emphasized trustful attributes of the topics on public agenda. We conclude that positive emotions within the Belgian Twitter discourse have the potential to increase the importance of positive aspects of the pandemicrelated agenda. In addition, the trust of the Belgian public in the ability to cope with the pandemic remained largely intact.

Like all research, this study has a number of limitations. First, the streaming Twitter API only provided a sample of up to one percent of all tweets. Although it seems likely that the sample is representative, Twitter does not disclose their sampling procedure and, therefore, it remains unknown. Second, although English, French, and Belgian sample data were collected, the analysis of the tweets content only regarded tweets in English. This might limit the possibility to infer how the Belgian Twitter users felt as a whole. Third, Belgian Twitter users are not an accurate representation of the Belgian public, and the tweets must be regarded as an indicative sample. Fourth, it is not clear to which extent the sample is subject to automated posting of tweets and not activity. These issues are a common concern within the use of Twitter data. 32 Despite its limitations, this study adds to the current state-ofthe-art Twitter analysis by being the first to examine the public discourse on Twitter in the first month of the SARS-CoV-2 crises. As such, it provides important insight into how the Belgian public responded to the far-reaching measures taken by the government to curtail public life.
